discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). The most famous street in the world — and Jacobs is clear-eyed about both its glory and its decline into car traffic and brand stores.

narrative.summary: The most famous street in the world — and Jacobs is clear-eyed about both its glory and its decline into car traffic and brand stores.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: The axial geometry from the Arc de Triomphe to the Place de la Concorde is still thrilling. The side allées with their chestnut trees, the width, the sense of formal procession. Best at dawn before the traffic, or understanding it as the upper half — the lower half (Tuileries to Concorde) retains the original park character.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.

blocks.name: Brasserie Fouquet's Paris
blocks.why: The most famous terrace on the Champs-Élysées. Founded 1899, historic monument since 1988. The grand brasserie that anchors the street's social theatre — Jacobs would see it as the edge institution that brings civic life to the boulevard.

blocks.name: Restaurant LE Drugstore
blocks.why: The Drugstore Publicis — landmark 1960s building by Michele Saee at the Arc de Triomphe end. Brasserie, cocktail bar, pharmacy, bookshop. Jacobs' active mixed-use edge condition in one address.

blocks.name: L'Alsace
blocks.why: Open 24 hours — one of the great Alsatian brasseries of Paris, on the lower half of the Champs. The street's nocturnal dimension: when the boulevard belongs to workers, insomniacs, and tourists who walk it for the light.
